content.problemStatement:
Chef prepared a problem. The admin has rated this problem for xxx points.

A problem is called :



Easy if 1≤x<1001 \leq x \lt 1001≤x<100




Medium if 100≤x<200100 \leq x \lt 200100≤x<200




Hard if 200≤x≤300200 \leq x \leq 300200≤x≤300



Find the category to which Chef’s problem belongs.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT denoting the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer xxx.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line the category of Chef's problem, i.e whether it is an `Easy`, `Medium` or `Hard` problem. **The output is case sensitive.**
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer x representing the rating of Chef's problem.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output on a separate line the category of the problem: "Easy", "Medium" or "Hard" (case‑sensitive).

sampleTestCases.explanation: x=50 falls in [1,100) → Easy; x=172 falls in [100,200) → Medium; x=201 falls in [200,300] → Hard.
x=100 is the lower bound of Medium (100 ≤ x < 200). x=200 is the lower bound of Hard (200 ≤ x ≤ 300).
99 is less than 100, so the problem is Easy.

solution.approach: Read the number of test cases. For each rating x, use a chain of if‑else statements: if x < 100 output "Easy"; else if x < 200 output "Medium"; otherwise output "Hard". This directly follows the definition of the three intervals.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – each test case is processed in constant time.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few integer variables are used.
